semantic_journey: Arabic al-qastanas = a type of wood (strong hard wood). Greek kastana = sweet chestnuts (Castanea sativa). The original correctly notes that Arabic al-qastanas is almost certainly a loanword from Greek kastanon (chestnut tree). Arabic is the borrower of the Greek botanical term. This is a documented Greek -> Arabic loanword, not an Arabic -> Greek cognate.
key_insight: Arabic al-qastanas is borrowed from Greek kastanon/kastana (chestnut); Arabic is the recipient of this Greek botanical loanword, not the source.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-qantaris = a plant name (cross-reference to centaury). Greek Kentauros = Centaur (mythological horse-man creature). The Arabic al-qanturiyun (centaury plant) is itself borrowed from Greek kentaureion (the plant named after the Centaur Chiron who used it medicinally). This is a Greek -> Arabic plant-name loanword. The Greek Kentauros is a proper mythological name. Both entries are: Arabic borrowed plant-name <- Greek; Greek mythological proper name. Shared loanword with Arabic as recipient.
key_insight: Arabic centaury plant name is borrowed from Greek kentaureion; Arabic is the recipient of this Greek loanword, not the source.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-karnas = horn-like protrusion (variant of al-qarnas, same root as GRC_0847). Greek koronopous = buck's horn plantain (named for horn-shaped leaves). Same analysis as GRC_0847. Core: horn/protrusion shape. 1-step direct preservation: horn-protrusion -> horn-shaped-leaf.
key_insight: Same analysis as GRC_0847; Arabic horn-protrusion and Greek horn-plantain share the horn shape as defining concept.

semantic_journey: Arabic al-kastyj = a thick cord/rope that Zoroastrians tie around their waist as a ritual belt (covering the body). Greek katastegos = covered in, roofed over (kata+stege = completely covered). Core: the concept of covering. Arabic encodes a ritual belt that covers/binds the body; Greek encodes being covered/roofed over. 1-step shared concept: covering/enclosing the body or structure.
key_insight: Arabic Zoroastrian ritual belt (body-covering/binding) and Greek covered-over share the concept of covering or enclosing.

semantic_journey: Arabic khawf = fear, dread. Greek ekpheugein = to flee out, to escape. Core: fear and flight are cognitively coupled. Arabic encodes the fear state; Greek encodes the flight response that fear produces. 1-step direct cause-to-result: fear -> flight-from-fear. This is the classic fear-flight pairing universal across languages.
key_insight: Arabic fear (khawf) and Greek fleeing/escaping are cognitively coupled as cause and immediate result; fear produces flight.

semantic_journey: Arabic khayf = fear, dread (variant of khawf). Greek ekpheugein = to flee out, to escape. Same analysis as GRC_0852. Core: fear -> flight. 1-step direct cause-to-result.
key_insight: Same as GRC_0852; Arabic fear (khayf) and Greek fleeing are cognitively coupled as fear produces flight.

semantic_journey: Arabic hindiky = Indian person (ethnonym for someone from India). Latin hindicus = Indian (adjective). Both are the same ethnonym for Indian origin. However, Arabic hindiky is itself derived from Hind (India), a name coming through Persian from Sanskrit Sindhu. Latin hindicus was borrowed from Arabic or from the same shared source. This is a shared ethnonym, not an Arabic original cognate.
key_insight: Arabic hindiky and Latin hindicus are the same ethnonym for Indian, both derived from Persian/Sanskrit Sindhu; this is a shared borrowed ethnonym, not an Arabic-Latin cognate.

semantic_journey: Arabic istaabl = stable for animals (borrowed from Latin stabulum). Latin stabilio = to stabilize, make firm (from stabilis = stable, from stare = to stand). The original correctly identifies that Arabic istaabl is borrowed FROM Latin stabulum. Comparing Arabic istaabl back to Latin stabilio is circular. Arabic is the loanword recipient.
key_insight: Arabic istaabl is borrowed FROM Latin stabulum; comparing it back to Latin stabilio is circular; Arabic is the recipient.
